section: Chapter 1: A Square and a Cube

questions.question: What is a perfect square?
questions.correct_answer: A number that can be written as the product of a number with itself
questions.explanation: Examples include 1, 4, 9, 16 which are squares of 1, 2, 3 and 4.

questions.question: What is meant by the square root of a number?
questions.correct_answer: A number which when multiplied by itself gives the original number

questions.question: What is a perfect cube?
questions.correct_answer: A number obtained by multiplying a number by itself three times

questions.question: What is the cube root of a number?
questions.correct_answer: A number which when multiplied by itself three times gives the original number

questions.question: How can square roots be found using prime factorisation?
questions.correct_answer: By grouping prime factors into pairs and taking one factor from each pair
questions.explanation: Each pair contributes one number to the square root.

questions.question: How can cube roots be found using prime factorisation?
questions.correct_answer: By grouping prime factors into sets of three
questions.explanation: Each group of three identical factors contributes one number to the cube root.

questions.question: Is 121 a perfect square?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, it is a perfect square

questions.question: Is 216 a perfect cube?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, it is a perfect cube

notes.title: Perfect Squares
notes.content: Perfect squares are numbers obtained by multiplying an integer by itself.

notes.title: Square Roots
notes.content: The square root of a number is the number that produces the given number when multiplied by itself.

notes.title: Perfect Cubes
notes.content: Perfect cubes are numbers obtained by multiplying an integer three times.

notes.title: Cube Roots
notes.content: The cube root of a number is the value which when cubed gives the original number.

notes.title: Prime Factorisation Method
notes.content: Prime factorisation can be used to find square roots and cube roots by grouping factors.

facts.fact: 1 is both a perfect square and a perfect cube.
The square of an even number is always even.
The cube of an odd number is always odd.
Perfect squares end with digits 0, 1, 4, 5, 6 or 9.
